name: The year of delegations, part 3
epithet: A false prophet, a distant miracle, and the curse no one would risk
title: The year of delegations, part 3: Musaylima, Yemen, and the Christians of Najran
meta_description: Day 96 of the seerah: the close of the year of delegations. The first false prophet, the miracle at Jurash, the praise of Yemen, and the Christians of Najran who declined the mutual curse, retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's series.
keywords: seerah, Prophet Muhammad, year of delegations, Musaylima, Banu Hanifa, false prophet, Najran, mubahala, Yemen, Muadh ibn Jabal, Yasir Qadhi seerah, life of the Prophet

video.title: Seerah of Prophet Muhammad 96 - The Year of Delegations - Part 3 ~ Dr. Yasir Qadhi | 12th Nov 2014

source_note: Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's Seerah of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, episode 96: the year of delegations, part 3 (Memphis Islamic Center). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is the Sheikh's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: The books of seerah list more than a hundred and ten delegations by name, and for most of them all we know is that they came. Over three days Dr. Yasir Qadhi has pulled out the ones worth lingering on, and on this third and final day he closes the arc with the strangest of them all: a delegation that did not come to submit, but to make a demand.

By the end you will have met the first man in Arab history ever to call himself a prophet, watched a miracle land in a far-off valley while two envoys stood confused in Madinah, and seen sixty Christians offered a curse so heavy they turned around and walked away from it. And running underneath all of it, one quiet astonishment: in a single year, the whole peninsula came.

sections.h2: The man who wanted half the prophethood
paras: Begin with Banu Hanifa, a large Christian tribe out of Yamama in central Arabia, and their leader, an elderly man named Musaylima. In his younger years he had travelled to Jerusalem to study Christianity, learned the language of the Romans, taken on their customs, and come home to decades of mounting respect. By the time Islam reached him he was perhaps in his late sixties, venerated by an entire region, and his men carried him into the Masjid shielded behind fine cloths, a procession fit for a king. Power had done to him what power does: he had come to believe people owed him something.

So he did not come to accept anything. His condition, set before he had even declared himself, was this: name me your successor and make me a partner in your prophethood, the way Musa made Harun a partner, and I will follow you. Otherwise, no. He had the audacity to say it to the Prophet's ﷺ face. The Prophet ﷺ was holding a branch from a tree, the way a person idly holds something while he speaks, and he answered: by Allah, if you asked me for this stick I would not give it to you. You want command after me, and I would not hand you this worthless branch. Allah will deal with you and humiliate you.

The Sheikh draws out a detail that gives this its weight. The Prophet ﷺ told Musaylima: you are the one Allah warned me about. Years later, when Ibn Abbas was an old man, he asked Abu Hurayra what dream that was. Abu Hurayra answered with a hadith preserved in Bukhari: the Prophet ﷺ said that in his sleep he saw two bracelets of gold on his arms, and gold is forbidden for men to wear, and the wrongness of it disturbed him. He was inspired to blow on them, and at his breath they broke off and flew away. He understood them to be two liars who would rise after him.

Musaylima was one of the bracelets. He became the first of the false prophets, and the Prophet ﷺ had foretold them: after me you will see liars, and the worst of them will follow others before them. The Sheikh pauses on a fact easy to miss: before this Prophet ﷺ, the Arabs had never even heard of such a thing as a man claiming prophethood. When Heraclius interrogated Abu Sufyan, one of his questions was whether prophets were common among them, and the answer was no, never. It was the success of the truth that summoned all the copycats. Musaylima even tried to imitate the style of the Qur'an, and his attempts were so hollow that the Sheikh notes one passing Arab, not yet a Muslim, told him plainly: by Allah, I know you are a liar. He was killed years later in the wars of apostasy, and the man who struck him was Wahshi, the same hand that had once killed Hamza, taking up his javelin to atone for what he had done.

Not every delegation came in arrogance. One of the tribes of Azd, a great people of Yemen, accepted Islam, and the Prophet ﷺ put one of his companions in charge of them with orders to bring in a neighbouring pagan tribe, the people of Jurash. While that was unfolding, Jurash, not yet Muslim, sent two envoys of their own to Madinah to take the measure of the Prophet ﷺ and learn whether he would grant a truce.

On the very day the two envoys stood before him, far away their tribe was under attack, news they could not possibly have had. The Prophet ﷺ turned to them and asked which tribe they were from. From Jurash, they said. And he told them: Allah's camel is being sacrificed at Jurash as we speak. They did not understand. Abu Bakr and another companion leaned in: do you not see what he means? Your tribe is being overrun. Your only hope is to beg him to ask Allah to save them. So they begged, and the Prophet ﷺ raised his hands and prayed for the people of Jurash to be guided. The two went home bewildered, and worked out that at the exact hour they had been speaking with him, their tribe had indeed been overcome and was on the edge of slaughter, until the Muslim commander chose to forgive and spare them. The whole tribe took it as a miracle, and the delegation that came next came not to negotiate but to embrace Islam.

Many tribes now came simply to learn: to memorise the Qur'an, to study the religion, to ask their deepest questions. The most striking question of all came from the Banu al-Harith of Yemen, in a hadith in Bukhari and Muslim. While a harsh northern delegation, the Banu Tamim, had answered the Prophet's ﷺ good news by asking instead for money, the men of Yemen answered differently: we accept this good news, and we have come all this way to ask you about the very beginning. How did Allah create it all? How did everything begin? The Prophet ﷺ told them there was Allah and there was nothing before Him, and He created the heavens and the earth while His Throne was upon the water. The Sheikh lingers here only to note one thing the wording quietly teaches: this world is not the only creation. There was already a Throne, already water, before the heavens and the earth were made.

The narrator of that hadith, Imran ibn Husayn, then heard someone shout that his camel had fled, and he ran after it, and lost both the camel and the rest of the hadith. How he wished afterward that he had let the camel go. It is part of why the people of Yemen drew such love from the Prophet ﷺ. The people of Yemen have come to you, he said, with the softest of hearts and the best of souls. Faith is Yemeni, and wisdom is Yemeni. And: O Allah, bless us in our Sham and in our Yemen, said at a time when neither land was yet under Muslim rule, calling them ours before they were.

sections.h2: Make it easy, do not make it hard
paras: A few months on, after Abu Bakr's Hajj, one of the kings of Yemen accepted Islam, and the Prophet ﷺ sent Muadh ibn Jabal to be his judge and deputy. He walked beside Muadh as Muadh rode, an honour in itself, giving him counsel for the road: you are going to a people of the Book, so call them first to the testimony of faith, and if they answer, then to prayer, and if they answer, then to charity. And near the end, gently, it is possible you will not see me after this year. It was, without either of them naming it, a farewell.

His last piece of advice to Muadh is one the Sheikh asks you to take personally. Make things easy and do not make them hard. Give people glad tidings and do not drive them away. Bring hearts together and do not split them. People are at different levels: meet them where they are, fill the gaps, draw them closer, and never turn them off the path with a truth spoken at the wrong moment to the wrong person. If Muadh, of all people, was sent with that instruction, the Sheikh asks, who are we to be harsh in the name of the religion?

ayah.arabic: إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ اللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ آدَمَ ۖ خَلَقَهُ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُ كُن فَيَكُونُ
ayah.text: Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created him from dust; then He said to him, "Be," and he was.

paras: Now the long one, and the most instructive. The Prophet ﷺ had written to Najran, a Christian province with close ties to Rome, addressing them in the name of the God of Ibrahim and Ishaq and Yaqub, and giving them three choices: accept Islam, pay the jizya, or stand and fight. They debated among themselves. Some said openly that a prophet had been foretold among the descendants of Ishmael, and perhaps this was him. So they sent a delegation of sixty, one of the largest ever, to judge for themselves.

They arrived dressed unlike any Arabs the companions had seen, and when the time for their own prayer came, the companions moved to stop them, and the Prophet ﷺ let them pray, turning to the east, in the Masjid itself. Then the dialogue ran for days. At the end of the first long day they put their hardest question: if you agree Isa was born of a virgin, then who was his father? The Prophet ﷺ did not answer from himself. He said: wait, and let Allah answer you. The next day came the opening of Surah Al Imran, and in it the answer that needs no father at all: the case of Isa before Allah is like the case of Adam, made from dust by a word, Be.

sections.h2: The curse no one would risk

ayah.arabic: فَمَنْ حَاجَّكَ فِيهِ مِن بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَكَ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ فَقُلْ تَعَالَوْا نَدْعُ أَبْنَاءَنَا وَأَبْنَاءَكُمْ وَنِسَاءَنَا وَنِسَاءَكُمْ وَأَنفُسَنَا وَأَنفُسَكُمْ ثُمَّ نَبْتَهِلْ فَنَجْعَل لَّعْنَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَى الْكَاذِبِينَ
ayah.text: Then whoever argues with you about it after [this] knowledge has come to you - say, "Come, let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, ourselves and yourselves, then supplicate earnestly [together] and invoke the curse of Allah upon the liars [among us]."

paras: When the arguing still would not end, Allah gave the final move, the mutual curse. If you are so certain you are upon the truth, then bring your children and your women and yourselves, and we will bring ours, and together we will invoke the curse of Allah upon whichever party is lying about Him. Both cannot be right: either Isa is the son of God or he is not. The Prophet ﷺ readied his own family, and the next morning the Christians of Najran withdrew. Among themselves they had reasoned that if he truly is a prophet and we do this, we will be wiped from the face of the earth. So they declined the curse and accepted a measured jizya instead, a fixed amount of cloth and silver each year, for as long as it suited both sides, and the Prophet ﷺ never bound a treaty forever.

The Sheikh draws the lessons gently, the way the day asks. The mutual curse is a last resort, never how you begin, and almost always a threat rather than a thing enacted. A senior figure among them privately admitted on the road home that this was the foretold prophet, and when his younger brother asked why then he had not believed, the man confessed it was for the honour and the wealth Rome had given them, and the younger brother was so shaken he left his people and became a Muslim. And note where Christians were allowed to pray: inside the holiest Masjid in Madinah, facing their own wall. The Prophet ﷺ tolerated the people of the Book and never forced them. He did not tolerate idol-worship, but a non-Muslim guest he honoured, fed, and housed, and let worship in his own house of worship, which is the true height of what the Sheikh calls realistic pluralism: clear about one truth, gentle with every soul.

dua.translit: Rabbana atina fid-dunya hasanah wa fil-akhirati hasanah, wa thabbit qulubana ala dinik
dua.en: Our Lord, give us good in this world and good in the next, and keep our hearts firm upon Your religion.

lessons_intro: The close of the delegations is a quiet handful of lessons, drawn straight from the way the Sheikh ends the year.

lessons.bold: Guard your heart from your own status.
lessons.text: Musaylima was not destroyed by an enemy but by his own importance. Power convinced him the world owed him something, even prophethood. Watch what a little reverence does to you ﷺ before it does it quietly.

The Prophet's ﷺ last word to Muadh was give glad tidings and do not drive people away. Meet people where they are. A truth spoken harshly at the wrong moment can close a door that gentleness would have opened.

lessons.bold: Truth is clear, but only for the willing.
lessons.text: Sincere people stood in front of the Prophet ﷺ himself and still did not believe. If they were not all convinced, the Sheikh says, do not break yourself when your own dawah does not land. Guidance is with Allah.

Christians prayed inside his Masjid ﷺ and went home unharmed with their treaty intact. We are clear that there is one way to Allah, and just as clear that no one is forced. Firmness and mercy are not opposites.

lessons.bold: The fruits of patience are sweet.
lessons.text: In a single year, with no armies marching, tribes came from every direction and the peninsula was united under one rule for the first time in human history. Allah kept His promise to him ﷺ. He always does.

faq.q: Who was Musaylima, and why is he called the first false prophet?
faq.a: Musaylima was the elderly leader of Banu Hanifa in Yamama. He came to the Prophet ﷺ not to accept Islam but to demand a share in prophethood and to be named successor. He was the first man in Arab history to claim prophethood, which the Prophet ﷺ had foreseen in a dream of two gold bracelets that broke and flew from his arms. Musaylima was later killed in the wars of apostasy by Wahshi.

faq.q: What was the miracle at Jurash?
faq.a: Two envoys from the pagan tribe of Jurash came to Madinah to seek a truce. On the very day they stood before the Prophet ﷺ, he told them their tribe was being overrun far away, news they could not have known. They begged him to pray, he prayed for their guidance, and they later confirmed that at that exact hour their people had been overcome, then spared. The tribe took it as a sign and embraced Islam.

faq.q: What did the Prophet ﷺ teach the people of Yemen about the beginning of creation?
faq.a: In a hadith in Bukhari and Muslim, the Banu al-Harith of Yemen asked how everything began. The Prophet ﷺ told them there was Allah and nothing before Him, and He created the heavens and the earth while His Throne was upon the water. The Sheikh notes this implies our world is not Allah's only creation, since the Throne and the water already existed.

faq.q: What was the mubahala with the Christians of Najran?
faq.a: After days of debate, Allah revealed Surah Al Imran 3:61, calling both sides to bring their families and invoke the curse of Allah upon whichever party was lying about Him. The Prophet ﷺ readied his family, but the Christians of Najran, fearing they would be destroyed if he truly was a prophet, declined the curse and accepted the jizya instead. The Sheikh stresses it is a last resort, not a way to begin dialogue.

faq.q: Were non-Muslims really allowed to pray inside the Prophet's ﷺ Masjid?
faq.a: Yes. The companions moved to stop the Christian delegation, but the Prophet ﷺ let them pray toward the east, within the Masjid, as guests. They were also fed and housed. The Sheikh draws this as a model of Islamic tolerance: clear that there is one path to Allah and that no one is forced, while honouring the guest who believes differently.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: The year of delegations ends not with a battle but with a doorway. Tribe after tribe walked in, the proud and the seeking, the pagan and the Christian and the half-convinced, and the whole peninsula was gathered under one rule for the first time in its history, won by patience rather than the sword. One man came to demand a share of prophethood and was offered not even a worthless stick. Sixty came to argue and were offered a curse they dared not touch. And many, many more came only to learn, to memorise, to ask, and to beg for a single du'a from him ﷺ.

Let that be the prayer you leave the day with. O Allah, keep our hearts firm upon Your religion as You kept the hearts of those who came to him from every horizon, make us easy on others as You commanded Your Prophet ﷺ to be, gather us among the people of the softest hearts and the best of souls, and let us drink from his hand a drink after which there is no thirst. Ameen.

slides:
Day 96 of 104.

The year of delegations closes. A false prophet, a far-off miracle, and sixty Christians offered a curse they would not risk.

Musaylima of Banu Hanifa came not to submit, but to demand half the prophethood.

The Prophet ﷺ, holding a branch: by Allah, I would not even give you this stick.

He became the first false prophet in Arab history.

The Prophet ﷺ had dreamed of two gold bracelets on his arms.

He blew on them, and they broke and flew away.

Two liars who would rise after him. Musaylima was one. (Bukhari)

Two envoys from Jurash stood before him ﷺ.

Allah's camel is being sacrificed at Jurash, he said, as your tribe is overrun, far away.

They begged him to pray. He did. They went home and found it true.

The people of Yemen came asking how it all began.

There was Allah, and nothing before Him.

The softest of hearts, the best of souls. Faith is Yemeni, and wisdom is Yemeni. (Bukhari, Muslim)

His last advice to Muadh, sent to Yemen:

Make things easy, do not make them hard. Give glad tidings, do not drive people away.

If Muadh was told this, who are we to be harsh?

Sixty Christians of Najran argued for days.

Allah called both sides to invoke His curse upon the liars.

They reasoned: if he truly is a prophet, we will be destroyed. So they walked away from the curse.

They prayed inside his Masjid ﷺ, toward their own wall, and went home with their treaty intact.

One truth, no force. Firm and merciful at once.

In one year, the whole peninsula came.
social.caption: Day 96 of 104: the year of delegations closes. The first false prophet who wanted half the prophethood and was not given a stick. A miracle that landed in a far valley while two envoys stood confused in Madinah. The praise of Yemen. And sixty Christians of Najran offered a curse so heavy they turned and walked away from it, then prayed inside his Masjid ﷺ and went home unharmed. In a single year, with no armies marching, the whole peninsula came. Retold faithfully from Dr. Yasir Qadhi's landmark seerah series (episode embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A new day of his life ﷺ, every day, at buruja.com. #seerah #ProphetMuhammad #Buruja #YasirQadhi #islam #sunnah #Najran

email.subject: The man who wanted half the prophethood
email.alt_subject: Sixty Christians, and a curse no one would risk
email.preview: Day 96 of 104: the year of delegations closes with its strangest visitors.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

The books of seerah list more than a hundred and ten delegations to the Prophet ﷺ. Most we know nothing about beyond the fact that they came. Today, on the third and final day of the year of delegations, Dr. Yasir Qadhi closes the arc with the ones worth remembering, and they are extraordinary.

First, Musaylima of Banu Hanifa, an old man drunk on decades of his own importance, who came not to submit but to demand a share in prophethood and to be named successor. The Prophet ﷺ, holding an idle branch, told him: by Allah, I would not give you even this stick. He became the first man in Arab history ever to call himself a prophet, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had foreseen in a dream of two gold bracelets that broke and flew from his arms.

Then a miracle at Jurash, announced in Madinah at the very hour it happened a world away. Then the people of Yemen, with the softest of hearts, asking how creation itself began. Then the Prophet's ﷺ farewell advice to Muadh, sent to Yemen: make things easy, do not make them hard, give glad tidings, do not drive people away.

And finally the Christians of Najran, sixty strong, who argued for days until Allah offered both sides a mutual curse upon whoever was lying about Him. They reasoned that if he truly was a prophet they would be destroyed, so they declined the curse, kept their religion, and went home in peace, having prayed inside his Masjid ﷺ with his blessing. One truth, no force.

In a single year, with no armies marching, the whole peninsula came. Read day 96, and watch the Sheikh's full episode, at the link below.
